dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 responding for a breathing problem at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Jake, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Um, I'm having trouble breathing and coughing for the last couple of days.
What is your name?
Can you open your eyes on command? Great, you're opening them on your own.
What's the current year and month?
Can you raise your left hand for me? And right? Thanks.
You're alert and oriented to who you are, where you are, what you are doing, and the time of day.
Can you talk with me? Your airway is patent.
Are you using extra muscles when breathing? Yes, that helps breathe better while seated.
I'll count your breaths now, just breathe normally.
Let me take your blood pressure. Is that okay?
Strong and equal pulses in both arms.
Heart rate is 85 at the moment.
Your oxygen level is 93-acceptable, but we'll monitor it closely.
What exactly is the trouble with breathing? When did this start?
I see you have a history of COPD - any recent worsening?
You mentioned Thursday is when you started. Any activity or position that made it better or worse?
Any chest pain or discomfort along with breathing or coughing?
Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color. Any swelling in the legs different from your normal level?
Let me check your stomach for tenderness or pain. Please let me know if anything feels uncomfortable.
Let's check your eyes. Are they normal and tracking? Yes, they are PERRL at 4mm.
I'm going to listen to your lungs. There is mild rhonchi in the left lower field and some adventitious sounds.
Still trying the monitor for blood sugar, but it isn't showing anything. We'll wait for the hospital.
ECG shows right bundle branch block, with a regular sinus rhythm.
We are placing you on a monitor and a 12-lead EKG to check for any changes.
We're putting you on 4 liters of oxygen through a nasal cannula to support your breathing.
I'm going to give you a Duoneb treatment with Albuterol and Ipratropium. Would that be okay?
With your COPD history and exacerbation, we'll follow the respiratory distress protocol.
Let's take your vitals again in a few minutes.
Blood pressure improved to 136/72, and breath sounds are calmer. Your oxygen level is now 94.
We'll be heading to [COMPANY_NAME]. Do you have any other preferences for where to go?
We're placing you on the stretcher in a semi-fowler's position with a shoulder and lap belt.
Just to check in, how do you feel now? Still no new symptoms or worsening.
It seems you are not experiencing any chest discomfort or pain now. Just keep focusing on slower breathing.
Your breathing rate dropped to 15 from 24-signs the medication is working.
EKG still shows a right bundle branch block, no acute changes. Capnography continues to show normal CO2 levels.
Heart rate is now 80, regular and strong. SpO2 at 95-your condition remains steady.
We'll start an IV in your arm to be ready in case of further need.
All set for transport. I'll give the emergency department a pre-arrival report now.
Your breathing has regular chest rise and fall without difficulty.
Equal strength and normal coordination in both arms and legs.
You mentioned surgery on your foot. Is the protective boot still on?
The surgical site is well covered and no signs of inflammation or redness.
Has this surgery made breathing or urination any different since?
You said you can't urinate since the surgery, right? You feel the urge but aren't able to.
You're in a tripod position to aid breathing. That helps you.
The Duoneb has finished. Your breathing seems more comfortable now.
You're secure for transport, and your airway is stable.
Everything looks good. We'll reach the hospital soon. Just breathe calmly.
Cap refill is brisk and skin remains dry with normal texture.
You're alert and oriented with a GCS of 15-no signs of confusion or new issues.
Some +1 edema in both legs, nothing more than what you usually have.
Less use of accessory muscles and breathing visibly easier than before.
Mild rhonchi in the left lower lung field. Right lung sounds are normal.
Eyes track well, no signs of drooping or unequal pupils.
Your SpO2 is 95% now. Supplemental oxygen is helping.
Still at 16 breaths per minute-no signs of increased labor or strain.
The transport unit is secure and you're stable for the ride to the hospital.
You're going to ED Room 16 at [COMPANY_NAME]. The staff is aware.
Another EKG confirmed right bundle branch block with no acute changes.
Our report is completed and handover is ready for the hospital emergency team.
IV is in place and working smoothly with no issues.
No new issues after starting the Duoneb and IV. You're ready to move.
We should arrive in 12 to 15 minutes. Try to breathe even slower if you can.
The IV is running at 60 mL/hr-clear and without wetness or swelling.
You had a recent meal, and no vomiting or nausea happened after.
I see no signs of jugular venous distention or masses in your neck.
No swelling or tenderness found in neck lymph nodes.
Given your past stroke, can you stick out your tongue or raise both arms? You did well.
No signs of new movement or sensation loss today.
EKG repeated and no new issues. Left anterior fascicular block still present.
Blood pressure is 126 over 69-still stable.
Hospital has our full report. I've observed and all is normal for now.
You didn't mention walking issues, just seated when I arrived.
You're alert and oriented with no signs of new confusion or stroke.
Pulse remains 81, regular and strong.
Normocephalic head. No rashes or unusual markings.
No more labored breathing overall. Your airway and breathing are secure.
Tripod posture is still good, and semi-fowler's is safe for travel.
Hospital is ready-handover is complete with your medical record.
No bad reactions from any current medications. Any known drug sensitivities? You're allergic to lorazepam only.
No increase in shortness of breath, dizziness, or numbness during the ride.
You seem fine. Let us know if you need help.
No tenderness in the abdomen. It's soft without guarding.
Primary transport planned. All equipment on board secured.
You had a recent negative COVID-19 test, correct? Have you had any contact since?
We're five minutes out. Oxygen and IV stable, EKG normal-no issues.
You're in great shape and the hospital is expecting you. Just relax and breathe.
Rechecking skin-still dry, no color or temperature changes.
No fever signs, and your temperature still stable since the last check.
All forms and report transferred to the ER team at [COMPANY_NAME].
Breathing improved, vitals stable, you're ready for emergency department care.
Final vitals: pulse 81, resp 16, BP 126/69, SpO2 95, EKG RBBB.
No other meds given. You were stable through the ride.
Ambulance is all set-meds, O2, and IV accounted for.
Clear for travel. No stops expected during transport.
All set. Moving to the hospital now.
